articles.title: Make science more reliable: study people as they go about their lives
articles.raw_summary: Nature, Published online: 22 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01957-z Researchers have long discussed the replication crisis. Now it’s time to tackle another problem in the behavioural sciences: generalizability.
articles.extracted_text: Thank you for visiting nature.com. You are using a browser version with limited support for CSS. To obtain the best experience, we recommend you use a more up to date browser (or turn off compatibility mode in Internet Explorer). In the meantime, to ensure continued support, we are displaying the site without styles and JavaScript. John A. List is a professor of economics at the University of Chicago, Chicago, Illinois, USA; at the Australian National University in Canberra, Australia; and chief economist at Walmart in Bentonville, Arkansas, USA. Natural field experiments might involve studying how shoppers respond to in-store changes, such as moving where products are displayed. Credit: Robert Nickelsberg/Getty Public policy is full of initiatives that did not work out as hoped. Take Scared Straight, a programme run by more than 30 US states between 1978 and 2015 that aimed to dissuade at-risk teenagers from becoming hardened criminals by bringing them face-to-face with people incarcerated in maximum-security prisons 1 , 2 . The programme was extended after a pilot project, the subject of a 1978 documentary, found that 80–90% of teenage participants stayed out of trouble. But the intervention did not work when scaled up. In some places, criminal behaviour among teenagers even rose. Similarly, many childhood-development interventions that have proved effective in one place have failed to deliver comparable results elsewhere. Deworming children in schools, for instance, substantially reduced absenteeism in Kenya but has shown mixed or weaker effects in other settings. School meal programmes in Burkina Faso increased student attendance but had limited impacts on outcomes in other countries 3 . Why science has a credibility problem — and how to address it Why science has a credibility problem — and how to address it This generalizability problem arises, in part, because human behaviour differs across populations and situations. People live in complex social environments in which labels, stakes and scrutiny shape every decision. But those contexts are often overlooked. Conventionally, research participants have come from Western, educated, industrialized, rich and democratic (WEIRD) populations 4 , which are unrepresentative of other groups on measures ranging from visual perception to moral reasoning and cooperation. What might work for them might not apply to other populations. In my view, one solution to the problem is to use a greater number of natural field experiments. In such studies, participants go about their everyday activities unaware that they are being observed by researchers, while some feature of their environment is varied. By studying people in their natural setting, assuming that strict ethical rules are followed, researchers can be more confident that their findings will be relevant to that group. Three developments make scientists better placed to take advantage of such approaches. First, attention on the replication crisis in academia has coincided with a growing understanding that results often fail to generalize beyond the narrow populations typically recruited for laboratory studies. Second, the technology sector is running thousands of natural field experiments to obtain reliable information about their customers, establishing infrastructure and methods that academics can use. articles.title: Cancer cells adopt unprecedented strategies to produce a molecule that protects them from iron-dependent death
articles.raw_summary: Nature, Published online: 22 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01802-3 The finding that spermine molecules in cells bind to iron to prevent it unleashing ferroptosis, a type of cell death, opens up strategies for treating tissue damage and cancer.

Amaia Zabala-Letona is at the Center for Cooperative Research in Biosciences, Basque Research and Technology Alliance, 48160 Derio, Spain, and at the Biomedical Research Network Center for Cancer, Madrid, Spain. Arkaitz Carracedo is at the Center for Cooperative Research in Biosciences, Basque Research and Technology Alliance, 48160 Derio, Spain; at the Biomedical Research Network Center for Cancer, Madrid, Spain; at Ikerbasque, Bilbao, Spain; and at the University of the Basque Country, Leioa, Spain. Human cells have built-in mechanisms that trigger their own death when intracellular damage or extracellular death signals accumulate. One such mechanism is ferroptosis 1 , by which free reactive iron-containing molecules inside cells react with cellular components (especially membrane lipids) to trigger the death of the cell. Ferroptosis contributes to tissue damage after events such as a temporary loss of blood flow, but it also limits the development or progression of diseases such as cancer 2 , 3 . Writing in Nature , Li et al. 4 reveal that a product of cellular metabolism (a metabolite) called spermine can trap iron, blocking this death program. This finding not only clarifies how cells defend themselves against damage, but also points to promising opportunities for treating tissue damage and cancer. articles.title: Why heritage sites are at risk in a warming world — and how to save them
articles.raw_summary: Nature, Published online: 22 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01956-0 As rising seas and intensifying disasters threaten historic sites worldwide, new ways to understand, preserve and adapt these places are needed urgently.
articles.extracted_text: Thank you for visiting nature.com. You are using a browser version with limited support for CSS. To obtain the best experience, we recommend you use a more up to date browser (or turn off compatibility mode in Internet Explorer). William Megarry is a reader in archaeology at the School of Natural and Built Environment, Queen’s University Belfast, Belfast, United Kingdom. The Moai stone figures on the coast of Rapa Nui (Easter Island) are one of many cultural sites that are at risk from climate-related pressures. Credit: Bas Vermolen/Getty The Future of the Past: When Cultural Heritage Meets Climate Change Thijs Weststeijn Polity (2026) In 2019, unprecedented flooding in Venice submerged more than 80% of the historic city, prompting Italy to declare a state of emergency. That same year — and in 2025 — wildfires in California burned thousands of hectares of land. The flames came dangerously close to the Getty Villa, a museum in Los Angeles that houses one of the world’s most important collections of ancient Greek and Roman art. These incidents illustrate a troubling reality: cultural heritage sites, once seen as enduring fixtures of human achievement, are increasingly vulnerable to climate-driven disasters. The idea that humanity’s greatest accomplishments are now threatened by our own failures is a theme that runs through art historian Thijs Weststeijn’s latest book. The Future of the Past offers a timely and accessible introduction to the growing tension between heritage preservation and a rapidly changing climate. Sustainability or dystopia? What past patterns tell us about where society is heading Sustainability or dystopia? What past patterns tell us about where society is heading Weststeijn lays out the scale of the challenge by examining cultural and historical sites around the world that are increasingly exposed to climate pressures, ranging from thawing permafrost to rising sea levels. One striking example is the Moai, the monumental stone figures of Rapa Nui (also known as Easter Island), many of which stand along the coast and are threatened by erosion and encroaching waters. More broadly, Weststeijn highlights a recurring paradox. Societies that once depended on the sea for mobility, trade and cultural exchange — such as those on the Swahili coast in East Africa — now find their heritage sites undermined by rising oceans and coastal instability. The very environments that enabled these cultures to flourish are becoming sources of risk. Alongside these case studies, Weststeijn situates the discussion in a broader intellectual history of heritage itself. He traces the origins of key concepts such as ‘world heritage’ — a term used by the United Nations cultural organization UNESCO to designate places on Earth that are of outstanding universal value to humanity — and shows how they emerged mainly from Western, Eurocentric frameworks. This is evident in his account of UNESCO’s founding in 1945 after the destruction of cultural monuments and artwork during the Second World War prompted an international effort to identify, value and protect such sites. He argues that although this framework has been influential, it can also limit how we understand and respond to cultural loss in a rapidly changing climate. articles.title: RBI Bulletin – June 2026
articles.raw_summary: Today, the Reserve Bank released the June 2026 issue of its monthly Bulletin. The Bulletin includes one speech, one article and current statistics. The article is on the State of the Economy. State of the Economy Geopolitical tensions and trade disruptions persisted, despite the recent interim peace deal in West Asia. Amidst the challenging global environment, the Indian economy grew at 7.8 per cent in Q4:2025-26, supported by private consumption and fixed investment. High-frequency indicators during the first two months suggest sustained economic momentum in 2026-27. Despite a pick-up in May, CPI-inflation remained anchored. India’s external sector remained resilient, supported by FDI inflows and adequate foreign exchange reserves. The views expressed in the Bulletin article are those of the authors and do not represent the views of the Reserve Bank of India. (Brij Raj) Chief General Manager Press Release: 2026-2027/514

articles.title: Freight Business Portal

Dedicated Freight Corridor Corporation of India Limited A Govt. of India (Ministry of Railways) Enterprise
DFCCIL Overview
articles.extracted_text: The Dedicated Freight Corridor Corporation of India Limited is India’s most ambitious and transformative infrastructure initiative undertaken by Indian Railways to modernize freight transportation and strengthen India’s logistics backbone. Conceived as a long-term structural solution to chronic congestion, declining rail share in freight movement and rising logistics costs, DFCCIL is creating high capacity, high speed, freight only rail corridors that are redefining how freight move across the country and supporting India’s journey towards sustainable, competitive and resilient logistics transportation. Eastern Dedicated Freight Corridor (EDFC) Length: 1,337 km Route: Ludhiana (Punjab) – Sonnagar (Bihar) Core Role: Freight corridor for energy and manufacturing sectors Western Dedicated Freight Corridor (WDFC) Route : Dadri (Uttar Pradesh) – JNPT (Mumbai) Core Role : High-capacity container logistics corridor Key Connectivity To strengthen first- and last-mile connectivity, DFCCIL is enabling development of Gati Shakti Cargo Terminals (GCTs) along the corridors. DFCCIL is one of the key driver of India’s green logistics transition, enabling a shift from road to rail through energy-efficient, high-capacity freight corridors. With significantly lower emissions, the Dedicated Freight Corridors support cleaner and more sustainable freight movement. DFCCIL promotes the use of renewable energy, contributing to long-term environmental sustainability , to further reduce its carbon footprint and advance India’s Net Zero goals. Engineering Marvels and Iconic Structures The Dedicated Freight Corridor network stands out as a landmark in modern railway engineering, delivering complex infrastructure across challenging geographies, dense urban stretches and environmentally sensitive zones. These corridors are engineered for heavy haul freight, high axle loads and long, high frequency trains, demanding a new benchmark in bridge design, tunnelling, alignment geometry and construction methodology. Iconic structures such as the Sohna Tunnel enable seamless freight movement through difficult terrain and advanced safety systems, while the Kalamboli Girder Bridge on the WDFC showcases precision engineering in a congested suburban environment without disrupting urban mobility. The Yamuna Rail Bridge near Dalelpur, Uttar Pradesh is one of India’s longest rail bridges, carries heavy haul freight traffic across the Yamuna River. DFCCIL provides a structural solution by creating dedicated, high-capacity corridors that separate freight from passenger traffic, ensuring faster, predictable and reliable movement of goods while releasing capacity on the existing network for passenger services. Project Funding and Financial Sustainability Beyond logistics efficiency, the Dedicated Freight Corridors have emerged as a powerful enabler of national priorities under the ‘PM Gati Shakti National Master Plan’. The corridors actively support flagship initiatives such Make in India, Start Up India, the Export Promotion Capital Goods Scheme and broader export promotion efforts by providing reliable, cost-effective logistics to manufacturing clusters and MSMEs. DFCCIL is instrumental in realising the vision of Aatmanirbhar Bharat by strengthening domestic supply chains, improving export competitiveness and integrating Indian industry with global value chains. As India aspires to become a developed nation by 2047, the DFCCIL stands as one of the foundational infrastructure pillar d

articles.title: Sonnagar - Dankuni
articles.extracted_text: Sonnagar- Dankuni Section The Sonnagar–Dankuni section, originally part of the Eastern Dedicated Freight Corridor (EDFC), spans approximately 538 km and traverses three states: Bihar, Jharkhand, and West Bengal. It passes through two zonal railways: East Central Railway (ECR) and Eastern Railway (ER). In 2022, the Sonnagar–Andal section was sanctioned as a multi-tracking project under the EPC mode and subsequently handed over to ECR and ER. The Andal–Dankuni section, covering 163 km, lies entirely within the state of West Bengal and falls under the jurisdiction of Eastern Railway. This section includes two junction stations (New Khana and New Dankuni), two crossing stations (New Rajbandh and New Jaugram), and one cabin at Andal East. As of now, approximately 97% of the required land for this section is under possession.

articles.title: New Corridor
articles.extracted_text: The proposed corridor stretching 1080 km traverses through three states of India, namely West Bengal, Orissa, and Andhra Pradesh. Mostly runs parallel to the existing Kharagpur-Vijayawada coastal railway line. It also passes over three zonal railways, namely South Eastern, East Coast and South-Central Railway. The DPR for this corridor is likely to be completed by Dec’22. Traffic Potential: The major traffic generators which will be contributing to East Coast Corridor in PIA are mines of Coal, Iron ore, Thermal Power Plants, Ports, Fertilizers, POL, CONCOR ICDs, FCI godowns, etc. 2. East West Corridor :- The proposed East-West DFC (EWDFC) from Bhusaval to Dankuni including spur line from Rajkharswan to Andal passes through five states namely West Bangal, Jharkhand, Odisha, Chhattisgarh, and Maharashtra. The length of the mainline is 1551 RKM and the length of the spur line is 187 RKM (Total: 1738 RKM). This corridor has further been extended from Bhusaval to Palghar. The DPR for this corridor is planned to be completed by Mar’23. Traffic Potential: The major traffic generators which will be contributing to East-West Corridor in project influence area (PIA) are Mines of Coal, Iron ore, bauxite, Manganese, Ferro Alloys, Steel Pants, Ports – major and minor, Fertilizers, Thermal Power Plants, Petroleum Oil Lubricants (POL), Cement plants, Container Corporation (CONCOR) Inland Container Deports (ICDs), Food Corporation of India (FCI) godowns etc. 3. North-South Corridor :- Traffic Potential: The major traffic generators which will be contributing to North-South Corridor in Project influence area (PIA) are Mines of Coal, Cement plants, Thermal Power Plants (TPP), Petroleum Oil Lubricants (POL), Container Corporation (CONCOR) Inland Container Deports (ICDs), Food Corporation of India (FCI) godowns, Fertilizers etc.

articles.title: Federal Reserve notes with deep sadness the passing of Alan Greenspan
articles.raw_summary: Federal Reserve notes with deep sadness the passing of Alan Greenspan
articles.extracted_text: An official website of the United States Government Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you've safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. The Federal Reserve, the central bank of the United States, provides the nation with a safe, flexible, and stable monetary and financial system. The Federal Reserve notes with deep sadness the passing of Alan Greenspan. Chairman Greenspan served as the 13th Chairman of the Board of Governors from 1987 to 2006, and his contributions to monetary policy and economic thought left a lasting mark on this institution, on the broader field of economics, and on the country. During his 18 years as Chairman, he guided the Federal Reserve through periods of significant economic expansion as well as periods of considerable stress. Under his leadership, the Federal Reserve achieved a sustained era of price stability that supported economic growth and helped anchor the public's confidence in the institution. He brought rigorous analytical discipline to monetary policymaking and helped establish the credibility that remains one of the Federal Reserve's most important assets. Chairman Greenspan's legacy endures at the Federal Reserveâin those he mentored directly, in the economists and public servants he inspired, and in the frameworks and practices he helped shape. The Federal Reserve extends its deepest condolences to his wife, Andrea Mitchell, and to his family. For media inquiries, please email [email protected] or call (202) 452-2955.

articles.title: Christine Lagarde: Hearing of the Committee on Economic and Monetary Affairs of the European Parliament
articles.extracted_text: It is a pleasure to return to this Committee for our regular dialogue. When we last met in February, inflation had remained close to the ECB’s 2% target for over a year, and euro area economic activity was showing solid growth momentum. Only a few days later, war broke out in the Middle East, reminding us how quickly external shocks can reshape the economic outlook. The topic you have chosen for today’s exchange – how monetary policy should be conducted in an environment of heightened geopolitical tensions and frequent supply shocks – is therefore particularly timely. In my remarks, I will first review what the incoming data tell us about the euro area economy and outline our latest monetary policy decisions. I will then explain in more detail how our monetary policy strategy is guiding us in responding to shocks such as the one we face today. The euro area economy was gaining some traction when the war in the Middle East broke out. Real GDP rose by 0.3%, quarter on quarter, in the first quarter of 2026, once adjusted for exceptional volatility in Ireland. However, the war is now weighing on activity, and incoming information points to a slowdown, especially in services. Manufacturing, meanwhile, has held up so far. This partly reflects inventory building in response to supply chain pressures, but also stronger defence spending. Looking ahead, the June 2026 Eurosystem staff projections see real GDP growth at 0.8% in 2026, 1.2% in 2027 and 1.5% in 2028. Staff now expect domestic demand to be weaker than they projected in March, as the war has dented confidence and higher energy costs are weighing on real incomes. At the same time, household balance sheets remain solid overall, and consumption should continue to be the main driver of growth. Investment should also be supported by firms spending on new digital technologies and governments spending more on defence and infrastructure. The war is likewise pushing up inflation, which rose to 3.2% in May, from 3.0% in April. The main driver of inflation since February has been rising energy inflation, which was above 10% in April and May. In May, inflation excluding energy and food also increased to 2.6%, partly reflecting the initial indirect effects of higher energy prices. Reflecting this energy spike, inflation expectations over shorter horizons have risen well above the levels seen before the outbreak of the war in the Middle East. But the public does not currently expect high inflation to be lasting: most measures of longer-term inflation expectations stand at around 2%, supporting the stabilisation of inflation around target in the medium term. We are therefore confident that, with appropriate monetary policy action, inflation will return to target. The June Eurosystem staff projections foresee headline inflation at 3.0% in 2026, 2.3% in 2027 and 2.0% in 2028. However, the outlook remains uncertain, with upside risks for inflation and downside risks for economic growth. The peace agreement in the Middle East is welcome, but the situation remains fragile, with risks of setbacks or re-escalation. The full implications of the war for medium-term inflation and growth will depend on the intensity and duration of the energy price shock, as well as the scale of its indirect and second-round effects. This uncertainty is reflected in the broad range of outcomes for inflation and growth in the alternative scenarios prepared by staff in the context of their projections and published on our website.

1.Industrial Production Operation in May 2026
articles.extracted_text: In May, the total value added of industrial enterprises above the designated size increased by 4.5% year on year in real terms (the growth rates of value added are the real growth rates after deducting price factors), 0.4 percentage points higher than the previous month. On a month-on-month basis, in May, the total value added of industrial enterprises above the designated size increased by 0.40% over the previous month. From January to May, the total value added of industrial enterprises above the designated size increased by 5.4% year on year. In terms of three sectors, in May, the value added of the mining industry increased by 2.3% year on year, that of the manufacturing industry increased by 4.4%, and that of the production and supply of electricity, heat power, gas and water increased by 7.6%. In terms of economic types, in May, the value added of state-holding enterprises increased by 3.7% year on year; that of share-holding enterprises increased by 5.2%; that of enterprises funded by foreign investors and investors from Hong Kong, Macao, and Taiwan increased by 1.9%; and that of private enterprises increased by 2.7%. In terms of industries, in May, the value added of 28 out of 41 major industries continued to show year-on-year growth. Among them, the value added of the mining and washing of coal went up by 3.5%, that of the extraction of petroleum and natural gas up by 1.5%, that of the processing of food from agricultural and sideline products up by 1.5%, that of the manufacture of liquor, beverages and refined tea down by 2.7%, that of the textile industry up by 2.6%, that of the manufacture of raw chemical materials and chemical products up by 0.3%, that of the manufacture of non-metallic mineral products down by 5.6%, that of the smelting and pressing of ferrous metals up by 1.6%, that of the smelting and pressing of non-ferrous metals down by 4.5%, that of the manufacture of general-purpose machinery up by 6.7%, that of the manufacture of special-purpose machinery up by 9.1%, that of the manufacture of automobiles up by 8.3%, that of the manufacture of railway, ship, aerospace and other transport equipment up by 7.4%, that of the manufacture of electrical machinery and apparatus up by 4.7%, that of the manufacture of computers, communication equipment and other electronic equipment up by 17.0%, and that of the production and supply of electricity and heat power up by 8.7%. In terms of products, in May, the output of 300 out of 626 products produced by industrial enterprises above the designated size increased year on year. Among them, the output of rolled steel was 123.03 million tons, down by 2.8% year on year; that of cement was 149.91 million tons, down by 8.1%; that of ten kinds of non-ferrous metals was 6.98 million tons, up by 2.2%; that of ethylene was 3.38 million tons, up by 2.1%; that of motor vehicles was 2.582 million sets, down by 3.2%, including 1.489 million new energy vehicles, up by 17.8%; the electricity generation was 784.3 billion kWh, up by 4.2%; and the crude oil processing volume was 53.72 million tons, down by 9.1%. In May, the product sales ratio of industrial enterprises above the designated size was 96.0%, a year-on-year decrease of 0.1 percentage points; and the export delivery value realized by industrial enterprises above the designated size was 1,388.4 billion yuan, a nominal increase of 10.1% year on year. Industrial Production Operation in May 2026 Value Added of Industrial Enterprises Above

articles.title: 2.Investment in Fixed Assets from January to May 2026
articles.extracted_text: From January to May 2026, the national investment in fixed assets (excluding rural households) was 17,851.2 billion yuan, a year-on-year decrease of 4.1% (calculated on a comparable basis, see Annotation 7 for details). Specifically, the investment in intellectual property products increased by 9.3% year on year. By industry, the investment in the primary industry was 388.7 billion yuan, a year-on-year increase of 5.9%; the investment in the secondary industry was 6,498.9 billion yuan, up 0.1%; and the investment in the tertiary industry was 10,963.6 billion yuan, down 6.8%. The industrial investment increased by 0.1% year on year. Specifically, the investment in mining increased by 6.2%, the investment in manufacturing decreased by 0.4%, and the investment in production and supply of electricity, heat power, gas and water decreased by 0.1%. The investment in infrastructure (see Annotation 1 for details of statistical scope) increased by 0.6% year on year. Specifically, the investment in waterway transportation increased by 23.3%, the investment in civil aviation transportation increased by 21.7%, the investment in ecological conservation and environmental governance increased by 3.5%, and the investment in the production and supply of gas increased by 2.8%. By region, the investment in the eastern region decreased by 6.4% year on year, the investment in the central region decreased by 2.9%, the investment in the western region decreased by 6.6%, and the investment in the northeastern region decreased by 17.5%. By statistical category of registration, the investment in fixed assets of domestic-invested enterprises decreased by 3.9% year on year, the investment in fixed assets of enterprises funded by investors from Hong Kong, Macao, and Taiwan decreased by 8.7%, and the investment in fixed assets of foreign-invested enterprises decreased by 4.3%. From January to May 2026, non-governmental investment in fixed assets decreased by 7.1% year on year. On a month-on-month basis, the investment in fixed assets (excluding rural households) decreased by 1.91% in May. articles.title: 3.Investment in Real Estate Development from January to May 2026
articles.extracted_text: I. The Completion of Investment in Real Estate Development From January to May, the investment in real estate development was 3,035.6 billion yuan, a year-on-year decrease of 16.2% (calculated on a comparable basis, see Annotation 5 for details), of which the investment in residential buildings was 2,342.6 billion yuan, down 15.6%. From January to May, the floor space of buildings under construction by real estate development enterprises was 5,487.75 million square meters, a year-on-year decrease of 12.3%. Specifically, the floor space of residential buildings under construction was 3,808.30 million square meters, down 12.6%. The floor space of buildings newly started was 179.29 million square meters, down 22.6%. Specifically, the floor space of residential buildings newly started was 130.84 million square meters, down 23.4%. The floor space of buildings completed was 140.87 million square meters, down 23.4%. Specifically, the floor space of residential buildings completed was 99.99 million square meters, down 25.0%. II. Sales of Newly Built Commercial Buildings From January to May, the floor space of newly built commercial buildings sold was 313.20 million square meters, a year-on-year decrease of 10.8%, of which the floor space of residential buildings sold decreased by 12.1%. The sales of newly built commercial buildings were 2,936.6 billion yuan, down 13.5%, of which the sales of residential buildings decreased by 14.1%. At the end of May, the floor space of commercial buildings for sale was 771.82 million square meters, down 0.4% year on year. Specifically, the floor space for sale held for less than three years was 571.52 million square meters, down 2.8%. III. Funds for Investment This Year for Real Estate Development Enterprises From January to May, the funds for investment this year for real estate development enterprises were 3,275.6 billion yuan, a year-on-year decrease of 19.0%. Among them, domestic loans were 487.5 billion yuan, down 28.7%; self-raised funds were 1,198.5 billion yuan, down 13.0%; deposits and advance receipts were 1,001.2 billion yuan, down 16.1%; individual mortgage loans were 406.6 billion yuan, down 28.0%. articles.title: 4.Total Retail Sales of Consumer Goods from January to May 2026
articles.extracted_text: From January to May, the total retail sales of consumer goods reached 20,603.1 billion yuan, up by 1.4% year on year. Specifically, the retail sales of consumer goods excluding automobiles reached 19,002.2 billion yuan, up by 2.7%. In May, the total retail sales of consumer goods reached 4,109.0 billion yuan, down by 0.6% year on year. Specifically, the retail sales of consumer goods excluding automobiles reached 3,778.1 billion yuan, up by 1.1%. By business area, from January to May, the retail sales of consumer goods in urban areas reached 17,866.2 billion yuan, up by 1.2% year on year; those in rural areas were 2,736.9 billion yuan, up by 2.6%. In May, the retail sales of consumer goods in urban areas reached 3,574.1 billion yuan, down by 0.9% year on year; those in rural areas were 534.9 billion yuan, up by 1.5%. By consumption pattern, from January to May, the retail sales of goods were 18,254.3 billion yuan, up by 1.2% year on year; the income of the catering industry was 2,348.8 billion yuan, up by 3.1%. In May, the retail sales of goods were 3,648.5 billion yuan, down by 0.7% year on year; the income of the catering industry was 460.5 billion yuan, up by 0.6%. By retail format, from January to May, the retail sales of convenience stores and supermarkets among retail enterprises (units) above the designated size increased by 6.8% and 3.6% year on year, respectively; those of specialty stores, department stores and brand-exclusive stores decreased by 1.2%, 1.8% and 7.6%, respectively. From January to May, Chinaâs online retail sales of goods and services reached 8,317.7 billion yuan, up by 5.9% year on year. Specifically, the online retail sales of goods were 5,271.8 billion yuan, up by 5.0%; among the online retail sales of goods, those of food, clothing, and daily necessities increased by 15.5%, 7.2%, and 1.6%, respectively. The online retail sales of services were 3,045.9 billion yuan, up by 7.6%. articles.title: 5.Energy Production in May 2026
articles.extracted_text: In May 2026, the raw coal production by industrial enterprises above the designated size remained at a relatively high level, the growth of crude oil production was steady, the growth of natural gas production fell slightly, and the growth of electricity generation accelerated. I. Raw Coal, Crude Oil and Natural Gas Production and Related Information Raw coal production remained at a relatively high level. In May, the raw coal production by industrial enterprises above the designated size was 400 million tons, down by 1.7% year on year; the average daily output was 12.81 million tons. From January to May, the raw coal production by industrial enterprises above the designated size was 1.98 billion tons, a year-on-year decrease of 0.3%. The growth of crude oil production was steady. In May, the crude oil production by industrial enterprises above the designated size was 18.57 million tons, a year-on-year increase of 0.5%, 0.7 percentage points lower than that in April; the average daily output was 599 thousand tons. From January to May, the crude oil production by industrial enterprises above the designated size was 91.31 million tons, a year-on-year increase of 1.1%. The decline in the processing volume of crude oil expanded. In May, the processing volume of crude oil by industrial enterprises above the designated size was 53.72 million tons, a year-on-year decrease of 9.1%, with the decline expanded by 3.3 percentage points from April; the average daily processing volume was 1.733 million tons. From January to May, the processing volume of crude oil by industrial enterprises above the designated size was 292.80 million tons, a year-on-year decrease of 2.2%. Natural gas production fell slightly. In May, the production of natural gas by industrial enterprises above the designated size was 21.7 billion cubic meters, a year-on-year decrease of 2.2%, compared with a 1.9% increase in April; the average daily output was 700 million cubic meters. From January to May, the production of natural gas by industrial enterprises above the designated size was 111.7 billion cubic meters, a year-on-year increase of 1.7%. Electricity generation by industrial enterprises above the designated size grew faster. In May, electricity generation by industrial enterprises above the designated size was 784.3 billion kWh, a year-on-year increase of 4.2%, with the growth rate 1.6 percentage points higher than that in April; the average daily electricity generation was 25.30 billion kWh. From January to May, electricity generation by industrial enterprises above the designated size was 3,912.9 billion kWh, a year-on-year increase of 3.6%. By category, in May, the growth of thermal power generation by industrial enterprises above the designated size slowed down, while that of hydropower and solar power generation accelerated; the growth of nuclear power and wind power generation turned from decline to growth. Among them, thermal power generation by industrial enterprises above the designated size increased by 2.1% year on year, with the growth rate 1.0 percentage point lower than that in April; hydropower generation increased by 13.0%, with the growth rate 0.8 percentage points higher than that in April; nuclear power generation increased by 5.0%, compared with an 8.7% decline in April; wind power generation increased by 0.5%, compared with a 5.0% decline in April; solar power generation increased by 12.1%, with the growth rate 5.0 percentage points higher than that i

articles.title: 6.National Economy Showed Steady Momentum with Innovation-led and High-quality Developmen...
articles.extracted_text: In May, under the strong leadership of the Central Committee of the Communist Party of China (CPC) with Comrade Xi Jinping at its core, all regions and departments fully implemented the decisions and arrangements made by the CPC Central Committee and the State Council, adhered to the general principle of pursuing progress while ensuring stability, fully and faithfully applied the new development philosophy on all fronts, moved faster to foster a new pattern of development, strictly implemented more proactive and effective macro policies, and effectively addressed external shocks and challenges. As a result, production and supply registered steady progress, employment and prices were generally stable, foreign trade demonstrated strong resilience, and new growth drivers witnessed robust development. The national economy maintained steady momentum with innovation-led and high-quality development. 1. Industrial Production Witnessed Accelerated Growth and Equipment Manufacturing and High-Tech Manufacturing Grew Fast. In May, the total value added of industrial enterprises above the designated size grew by 4.5 percent year on year, 0.4 percentage points faster than that of the previous month; the month-on-month growth was 0.40 percent. In terms of sectors, the value added of mining increased by 2.3 percent year on year, that of manufacturing grew by 4.4 percent, and that of production and supply of electricity, heat power, gas and water went up by 7.6 percent. The value added of equipment manufacturing increased by 9.5 percent year on year and that of high-tech manufacturing increased by 15.1 percent, 1.2 percentage points and 2.3 percentage points faster than that of the previous month respectively. An analysis by types of ownership showed that the value added of state holding enterprises was up by 3.7 percent year on year; that of share-holding enterprises was up by 5.2 percent; that of enterprises funded by foreign investors or investors from Hong Kong, Macao and Taiwan was up by 1.9 percent; and that of private enterprises was up by 2.7 percent. In terms of products, the production of 3D printing devices, lithium-ion batteries and industrial robots grew by 54.4 percent, 40.0 percent and 27.9 percent year on year respectively. In the first five months, the value added of industrial enterprises above the designated size went up by 5.4 percent year on year. In May, the Manufacturing Purchasing Managersâ Index stood at 50.0 percent. The Production and Operation Expectation Index was 53.9 percent. In the first four months, the total profits made by industrial enterprises above the designated size were 2,435.8 billion yuan, up by 18.2 percent year on year. 2. Service Sector Grew Steadily and Modern Services Showed Good Momentum of Development. In May, the Index of Services Production grew by 4.4 percent year on year, 0.1 percentage points faster than that of the previous month. Specifically, that of information transmission, software and information technology services, leasing and business services, finance, and transport, storage and postal services grew by 11.3 percent, 10.9 percent, 7.0 percent and 4.8 percent year on year respectively. In the first five months, the Index of Services Production increased by 4.8 percent year on year. In the first four months, the business revenue of service enterprises above the designated size grew by 6.4 percent year on year. In May, the Business Activity Index for Services stood at 50.3 percent and the

articles.title: 7.Sales Prices of Commercial Residential Buildings in 70 Medium and Large-sized Cities in...
articles.extracted_text: Sales Price Indices of Newly Constructed Commercial Residential Buildings in 70 Large and Medium-Sized Cities Sales Price Indices of Second-Hand Residential Buildings in 70 Large and Medium-Sized Cities Price Indices of Newly Constructed Commercial Residential Buildings by Floor Space in 70 Large and Medium-Sized Cities Price Indices of Second-Hand Residential Buildings by Floor Space in 70 Large and Medium-Sized Cities 1. Survey Coverage: The survey was conducted in the municipal districts of 70 medium and large-sized cities, excluding the counties. 2. Survey Methods: The survey of newly constructed commercial residential buildingsâ sales prices is an overall survey, with basic data derived from online contract-signing data provided by local real estate management departments. The survey of second-hand residential buildingsâ sales prices combines the survey of main units with typical survey, with basic data sourced from reports submitted by real estate agencies or housing service platforms, as well as on-site price collection by surveyors. 3. The calculation of the price indices can be seen in the Statistical Reporting System on Real Estate Prices. 4. If there is no deal record this month, the overall price level is regarded as unchanged. 5. According to the requirements of the statistical system, starting from January 2026, the statistical survey of real estate prices in 70 medium and large-sized cities takes 2025 as the base period for a new round of comparison. The weights of basic categories for each city have been adjusted to better reflect changes in the residential building sales structure. According to the calculation, the impact of the base period rotation on the monthly year-on-year sales price indices of commercial residential buildings in 70 medium and large-sized cities is within 0.03 percentage points on average.

articles.title: 9.Consumer Price Index in May 2026
articles.extracted_text: In May 2026, Chinaâs Consumer Price Index (CPI) increased by 1.2% year on year. Specifically, the price index in urban areas increased by 1.3% while that in rural areas increased by 1.1%; the price index for food decreased by 1.7% while that for non-food increased by 1.9%; the price index for consumer goods increased by 1.6% while that for services increased by 0.8%. From January to May, on average, Chinaâs CPI increased by 1.0% year on year. In May, Chinaâs CPI decreased by 0.1% month on month. Specifically, the price index in urban areas decreased by 0.1% while that in rural areas decreased by 0.1%; the price index for food decreased by 0.4% while that for non-food decreased by 0.1%; the price index for consumer goods decreased by 0.2% while that for services decreased by 0.1%. I. Year-on-Year Changes in Price Indexes for Various Goods and Services In May, the price index for food, tobacco, liquor and dining out decreased by 0.9% year on year, bringing the CPI down by about 0.26 percentage points. Among food, the price index for meat of livestock decreased by 7.4%, bringing the CPI down by about 0.31 percentage points, of which the price index for pork decreased by 16.1%, bringing the CPI down by about 0.31 percentage points; that for fresh fruits decreased by 2.2%, bringing the CPI down by about 0.04 percentage points; that for eggs increased by 6.6%, pushing the CPI up by about 0.03 percentage points; that for fresh vegetables increased by 1.6%, pushing the CPI up by about 0.02 percentage points; that for aquatic products increased by 0.6%, pushing the CPI up by about 0.01 percentage points. Among the price indexes for the other seven major categories, six increased, and one decreased year on year. Specifically, the price indexes for miscellaneous goods and services, transportation and telecommunication, and health care and medical services increased by 9.9%, 5.4% and 2.1%, respectively; the price indexes for household facilities, articles and services, clothing and education, culture and recreation increased by 1.8%, 1.4% and 1.3%, respectively; the price index for residence decreased by 0.2%. II. Month-on-Month Changes in Price Indexes for Various Goods and Services In May, the price index for food, tobacco, liquor and dining out decreased by 0.2% month on month, bringing the CPI down by about 0.06 percentage points. Among food, the price index for fresh vegetables decreased by 3.6%, bringing the CPI down by about 0.06 percentage points; that for meat of livestock decreased by 0.8%, bringing the CPI down by about 0.03 percentage points, of which the price index for pork decreased by 1.6%, bringing the CPI down by about 0.03 percentage points; that for eggs increased by 5.0%, pushing the CPI up by about 0.03 percentage points; and that for fresh fruits increased by 0.6%, pushing the CPI up by about 0.01 percentage points. Among the price indexes for the other seven major categories, one increased, four decreased and two remained flat month on month. Specifically, the price index for clothing increased by 0.6%; the price indexes for education, culture and recreation, and health care and medical services remained flat; the price indexes for household facilities, articles and services and miscellaneous goods and services both decreased by 0.4%; the price indexes for transportation and telecommunication and residence decreased by 0.3% and 0.1%, respectively. articles.title: 10.Industrial Producer Price Indexes in May 2026
articles.extracted_text: In May 2026, Chinaâs producer price index for industrial products (PPI) increased by 3.9% year on year and 0.5% month on month. The purchasing price index for industrial producers increased by 5.8% year on year and 1.3% month on month. From January to May, on average, the producer price index for industrial products increased by 1.0% year on year, and the purchasing price index for industrial producers increased by 1.6%. I. Year-on-Year Changes in Industrial Producer Price Indexes In May, among producer price indexes for industrial products, the price index for means of production increased by 5.2% year on year, pushing the overall producer price index for industrial products up by about 4.08 percentage points. Specifically, the price index for mining and quarrying increased by 15.8%, that for raw materials increased by 9.2%, and that for the processing industry increased by 2.3%. The price index for consumer goods decreased by 0.8%, bringing the overall producer price index for industrial products down by about 0.18 percentage points. Specifically, the price index for food decreased by 1.8%, that for clothing and articles for daily use both decreased by 1.0%, and that for durable consumer goods remained flat. Among purchasing price indexes for industrial producers, the price index for nonferrous metals and cables increased by 22.0%, that for raw chemical materials increased by 11.8%, that for fuel and power increased by 10.0%, that for textile materials increased by 2.5%, that for ferrous metals increased by 0.3%, that for building materials and non-metals decreased by 5.5%, and that for agricultural and sideline products decreased by 1.6%. II. Month-on-Month Changes in Industrial Producer Price Indexes In May, among producer price indexes for industrial products, the price index for means of production increased by 0.7% month on month, pushing the overall producer price index for industrial products up by about 0.53 percentage points. Specifically, the price index for mining and quarrying increased by 1.5%, that for raw materials increased by 0.9%, and that for the processing industry increased by 0.5%. The price index for consumer goods remained flat. Specifically, the price index for food decreased by 0.1%, and that for clothing, articles for daily use and durable consumer goods all increased by 0.1%. Among purchasing price indexes for industrial producers, the price index for raw chemical materials increased by 4.2%, that for fuel and power increased by 2.7%, that for textile materials increased by 1.0%, that for ferrous metals increased by 0.6%, that for nonferrous metals and cables and for agricultural and sideline products both increased by 0.3%, and that for building materials and non-metals both decreased by 0.5%.
